Makefile: Verilator + UVM_HOME compile RTL, interface, and test_*.sv UVM phases: build → connect → run_test → report (same pattern as Module 2) Sequence → driver → DUT → monitor → scoreboard (read SCOREBOARD at end) Typical command: make SIM=verilator TEST=test_* (see module1 demo slide) Loopback / hooks connect... |

| 17 | Directed test execution sequence | 0:28 | Follow these steps in order when working through this module. make SIM=verilator TEST=test_and_gate compile run read result Follow this order when tracing waveforms or debugging. |
| 18 | Verification & testing methods | 0:08 | Next section: Verification & testing methods. |
| 19 | 1. Directed self-check methodology | 0:38 | 1. Directed self-check methodology. Instantiate DUT, apply known stimulus in initial block or C++ main Compare outputs in-test; print PASS / FAIL (no external scoreboard yet) Use $display / $error for visibility; graduate to structured logging in Module 2 Simulation flow: make SIM=verilator TEST=test_and_gate → compile → run → read result Refer to the diagram on the right. |
| 20 | 2. Baseline test closure (1/2) | 0:38 | 2. Baseline test closure (1/2). Stimulus: fixed vectors covering corner cases (0, 1, overflow, reset) Checking: inline assertions and explicit comparisons against expected values Coverage: manual review of cases exercised (functional coverage in Module 5) Regression: ./scripts/module1.sh --all-sv runs all example compilations Refer to the diagram on the right. |
| 21 | 2. Baseline test closure (2/2) | 0:16 | 2. Baseline test closure (2/2). Out of scope: constrained-random, scoreboards, multi-agent coordination (later modules) |

| 34 | Detail: Functions vs Tasks (1) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Functions vs Tasks (1). Purpose: Compute and return a single value Return Type: Must declare a return type (void, int, string, bit, etc.) Timing: Execute in zero simulation time (no delays allowed) Usage: For calculations, data transformations, comparisons From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |

Syntax: function <return_type> <name>(<arguments>); ... endfunction When to Use: ✅ Data validation (is_valid_address()) ✅ Calculations (calculate_checksum()) From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |

| 37 | Detail: Built-in SystemVerilog Functions (2) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Built-in SystemVerilog Functions (2). %0b - Binary %0s - String %0t - Time %0f - Real (floating point) From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 38 | Detail: Class Methods (Functions in Classes) (1) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Class Methods (Functions in Classes) (1). Purpose: Initialize object when created Called: Automatically when using new() operator Parameters: Can have default values Example: Transaction txn = new(); creates object and calls new() From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 39 | Detail: Class Methods (Functions in Classes) (2) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Class Methods (Functions in Classes) (2). Purpose: Create deep copy of object Usage: Duplicate transactions for scoreboards, queues Null Check: Always check for null before accessing object fields Example: txn_copy.copy(txn1); copies all fields from txn1 to txn_copy From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 40 | Detail: Tasks in Testbenches (1) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Tasks in Testbenches (1). Purpose: Organize test code into reusable units Timing: Can contain delays (#10) and event waits (@(posedge clk)) Usage: Each test case is a separate task Calling: test_basic(); executes the task From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 41 | Detail: Tasks in Testbenches (2) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Tasks in Testbenches (2). Purpose: Encapsulate common sequences (reset, initialization) Reusability: Can be called from multiple test tasks Usage: reset(); applies reset sequence before each test Purpose: Pass data to tasks From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 42 | Detail: Array Methods (1) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Array Methods (1). push_back(): Add element to end (FIFO enqueue) pop_front(): Remove element from front (FIFO dequeue) size(): Return number of elements Usage: Transaction queues, scoreboards From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 43 | Detail: Array Methods (2) | 0:24 | Protocol and design detail: Array Methods (2). exists(): Check if key exists in associative array Indexing: Use any integral type as key Usage: Scoreboards, coverage collectors From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 44 | Detail: Function and Task Best Practices (1) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Function and Task Best Practices (1). Functions execute in zero time Perfect for data transformations, comparisons Must return a value (or void) Tasks can contain delays From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 45 | Detail: Function and Task Best Practices (2) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Function and Task Best Practices (2). Perfect for test sequences, stimulus generation No return value Always check for null before accessing object fields Prevents simulation crashes From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 46 | Detail: Real-World Testbench Examples (1) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Real-World Testbench Examples (1). Uses delays (#10) for signal propagation Uses assert with $error() for verification Uses $display() for test output Organized as separate task for reusability From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 47 | Detail: Real-World Testbench Examples (2) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Real-World Testbench Examples (2). Helper task (reset()) encapsulates common sequence Uses @(posedge clk) for clock synchronization Combines delays and event control Uses type casting (8'(i)) for bit width matching From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 48 | Detail: Performance Considerations (1) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Performance Considerations (1). Functions: Execute in zero time, no simulation overhead Tasks: Consume simulation time, have scheduling overhead Recommendation: Use functions for calculations, tasks only when timing is needed push_back() / pop_front(): O(1) for queues From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 49 | Detail: Performance Considerations (2) | 0:24 | Protocol and design detail: Performance Considerations (2). size(): O(1) for queues and arrays exists(): O(1) average for associative arrays foreach: O(n) where n is number of elements From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 50 | Detail: Troubleshooting Guide (1) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Troubleshooting Guide (1). Cause: Trying to use #, @, or wait in a function Solution: Convert to task or remove timing control Cause: Accessing object fields without null check Solution: Always check if (obj == null) before accessing From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 51 | Detail: Troubleshooting Guide (2) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Troubleshooting Guide (2). Cause: Trying to use task return value: int x = task_name(); Solution: Use output parameter: task_name(output int x); Cause: Function declared with return type but no return statement Solution: Add return statement or change to void function From MODULE1 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 52 | Detail: Summary (1) | 0:28 | In summary: Understand SystemVerilog for verification and verification fundamentals Next up: Next module in course. | 101 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (8/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (8/26). Purpose: Format string with placeholders (like sprintf in C) Returns: Formatted string (doesn't print to console) Usage: Create string representations of objects Example from code: Used in convert2string() methods to format transaction data Real Example (from transaction.sv): |
| 102 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (9/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (9/26). Usage: End testbench execution after tests complete Exit Codes: 0 = success, 1 = error, 2 = fatal Example from code: Called at end of all test programs Real Example (from test_and_gate.sv): Purpose: Report errors with formatted messages |
| 103 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (10/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (10/26). Severity: ERROR level (stops simulation if severity is high enough) Example from code: Used in test assertions when checks fail Real Example (from test_and_gate.sv): Purpose: Different severity levels for reporting Usage: Use appropriate level for the situation |
| 104 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (11/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (11/26). Purpose: Get current simulation time Usage: Timing verification, logging with timestamps Purpose: Generate random numbers for test stimulus Usage: Random test generation, constrained random verification Example from code: Used in error_handling_example.sv for retry simulation |
| 105 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (12/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (12/26). Returns: 1 if cast succeeds, 0 if fails Usage: Polymorphic object handling, type checking Purpose: Initialize object when created Called: Automatically when using new() operator Parameters: Can have default values |
| 106 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (13/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (13/26). Purpose: Create deep copy of object Usage: Duplicate transactions for scoreboards, queues Null Check: Always check for null before accessing object fields Example: txn_copy.copy(txn1); copies all fields from txn1 to txn_copy Purpose: Compare two objects for equality |
| 107 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (14/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (14/26). Usage: Scoreboard checking, test verification Example: if (txn1.compare(txn2)) $display("Match!"); Purpose: Create human-readable string representation Returns: Formatted string with object fields Usage: Debugging, logging, test output |
| 108 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (15/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (15/26). Purpose: Provide controlled access to private fields Usage: Encapsulation, data access Example: int id_val = txn.get_id(); Purpose: Organize test code into reusable units Timing: Can contain delays (#10) and event waits (@(posedge clk)) |
| 109 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (16/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (16/26). Calling: test_basic(); executes the task Purpose: Encapsulate common sequences (reset, initialization) Reusability: Can be called from multiple test tasks Usage: reset(); applies reset sequence before each test Purpose: Pass data to tasks |
| 110 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (17/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (17/26). Calling: write_transaction(16'h1000, 32'hDEADBEEF); push_back(): Add element to end (FIFO enqueue) pop_front(): Remove element from front (FIFO dequeue) size(): Return number of elements Usage: Transaction queues, scoreboards |
| 111 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (18/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (18/26). Indexing: Use any integral type as key Usage: Scoreboards, coverage collectors Functions execute in zero time Perfect for data transformations, comparisons Must return a value (or void) |
| 112 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (19/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (19/26). Perfect for test sequences, stimulus generation No return value Always check for null before accessing object fields Prevents simulation crashes Derived classes can override base class methods |
| 113 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (20/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (20/26). Each test case as separate task Main initial block orchestrates test execution Improves code readability and maintainability Uses delays (#10) for signal propagation Uses assert with $error() for verification |
| 114 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (21/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (21/26). Organized as separate task for reusability Helper task (reset()) encapsulates common sequence Uses @(posedge clk) for clock synchronization Combines delays and event control Uses type casting (8'(i)) for bit width matching |
| 115 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (22/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (22/26). Type casting with bit'(i) Expression evaluation in assertion Multiple format specifiers in $error() Functions: Execute in zero time, no simulation overhead Tasks: Consume simulation time, have scheduling overhead |
| 116 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (23/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (23/26). push_back() / pop_front(): O(1) for queues size(): O(1) for queues and arrays exists(): O(1) average for associative arrays foreach: O(n) where n is number of elements Cause: Trying to use #, @, or wait in a function |
| 117 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (24/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (24/26). Cause: Accessing object fields without null check Solution: Always check if (obj == null) before accessing Cause: Trying to use task return value: int x = task_name(); Solution: Use output parameter: task_name(output int x); Cause: Function declared with return type but no return statement |
| 118 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (25/26) | 0:36 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (25/26). Cause: Using wrong format in $display() or $sformatf() Solution: Use %0d for decimal, %0h for hex, %0b for binary, %0s for string Functions: Zero-time operations that return values; use for calculations Tasks: Time-consuming operations; use for test sequences Class Methods: Functions within classes; enable object-oriented programming |
| 119 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (26/26) | 0:28 | 6. SystemVerilog Functions and Tasks (26/26). Array Methods: push_back(), pop_front(), size(), exists() Best Practices: Null checks, method overriding, task organization Common Pitfalls: Delays in functions, missing null checks, wrong assignment types Performance: Functions are faster; use tasks only when timing is needed |

        ## Section narration (edit for TTS)

        - **Design architecture (Repository and example layout, Verification building blocks):** Walk through the block diagram, then relate each block to files under module1/examples/.
- **Execution:** Explain make run / UVM make steps, then walk the artifact table and directed-test sequence slide by slide.
- **Verification (Directed self-check methodology, Baseline test closure):** Explain what stimulus is applied, what is checked, and what is intentionally out of scope.
- **Syllabus:** Cover 6 topic section(s) — pause on protocol timing and signals.
- **Before exercises:** Ask learners to recall the learning outcomes slide; they should explain each bullet in their own words.
- **Hands-on:** Run module1/EXAMPLES.md labs; narrate expected PASS lines.

        ## Notes

        - Slides from **Before You Start**, **Design Architecture**, **Verification & Testing Methods**, **Topics Covered**, **EXAMPLES.md**, and **Learning Outcomes**.
        - Full detail: `docs/MODULE1.md` and `module1/EXAMPLES.md`.
        - Regenerate: `regenerate_course_outlines.sh <course_root> --module 1`
